attack_summary: A compromised internal Ubuntu host (UbuntuComputer, 192.168.1.3) under attacker control (server 203.0.113.1) executed an active ICMP flood (hping3) against the edge/gateway (7.0.14.254) to cause a network denial-of-service (T1498.001). The agent also performed discovery (ping to LAN 192.168.1.2) and internet connectivity checks. The attacker remained active and host removal/isolation was not allowed.

mitigations.name: Insert inline Snort IDS/IPS between FortiGate and LAN switch
mitigations.brief_description: Deleted the existing FortiGate<->switch link, added a pre-configured Snort IPS (snort-ids-1) and reconnected FortiGate -> Snort -> Cisco switch. The inline Snort inspected and blocked the malicious ICMP flood while preserving LAN and Internet connectivity.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

key_observations: Attack vector: outbound ICMP flood originated from a compromised internal host (UbuntuComputer) using hping3 to flood 7.0.14.254.
Inline IPS insertion (FortiGate -> Snort -> switch) is effective in this topology to stop ICMP floods without disconnecting the infected host or blocking legitimate traffic.
Validation showed attack success rate fell from 1.0 to 0.0 and legitimate checks (LAN ping and HTTP to 1.1.1.1) remained functional.
Mitigation depends on Snort's pre-configured rule set; gaps are possible if attacker changes flood characteristics or switches to other protocols (TCP/UDP) or evasion techniques.
All actions respected live-response constraints: the compromised host remained on-network and no host-level remediation (isolation/reimage) was performed.
overall_conclusion: Placing a pre-configured inline Snort IPS between the firewall and internal switch successfully stopped the active ICMP flood while preserving legitimate connectivity and complying with live-response constraints; however, continued monitoring and additional controls (egress filtering, firewall rate-limits, telemetry) are recommended because protection relies on the Snort rule set and the attacker may change tactics.
